coredata.dc:title: Bright and photostable MHA derived “luminous pearls” for multi-color bioimaging: An eco-sustainable cradle-to-gate approach guided by GRA coupled ANN
coredata.dc:creator: Dash, Priyanka
Pattanayak, Suvranshu
Ghosh, Sayantan
Ranjan Das, Nihar
Bhakuni, Pramod
Roy Barman, Sudipto
Nayak, Bismita
coredata.dc:description: In the Nano era, the creative drive of artificial intelligence (AI) focuses on the controllable synthesis of materials with the minimum number of experimental iterations. This innovation unlocks new horizons in the avenue of materiobiology and biomedicine. The multifarious exploration of luminescent nanodots and their pliable association with versatile imaging techniques have made impressive strides in the biomedical realm, particularly in onco-theranostics. Enlarged nuclei indicate tumor proliferation, and these manifestations can be rendered visible with the aid of fluorescence (FL) imaging. Herein, we present the “cradle-to-gate” transformation of Mueller Hinton agar (MHA) into a biological probe with alluring physicochemical attributes. More importantly, a comprehensive understanding of how each parameter intricately shapes the properties of nanomaterials remains elusive. To bridge this gap, mathematically derived grey relational analysis (GRA) and a soft-computing-based modelling tool-artificial neural network (ANN) were proposed in this work to impose stringent control on the fabrication of ultra-small (∼3 nm) nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) with high quantum yield (QY). NCQDs demonstrated remarkable antijamming performance and high photostability, making them ideal for bioimaging. Moreover, the water-soluble luminous pearls served as a biocompatible trident in cancer biology, achieving a three-pronged action of in-vitro cell photostability, multi-color imaging, and migration without hampering the biological system. The histological and biochemical analysis demonstrated no overt toxicity of NCQDs in mice, even under multi-dosing situations. The convergence of experimental and simulation results vouch for the potential fabrication of a simple and powerful “diagnostic” nanoplatform for highly effective medical diagnosis. This breakthrough offers substantial biomedical applications in the future.

head.ce:title: Bright and photostable MHA derived “luminous pearls” for multi-color bioimaging: An eco-sustainable cradle-to-gate approach guided by GRA coupled ANN

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the Nano era, the creative drive of artificial intelligence (AI) focuses on the controllable synthesis of materials with the minimum number of experimental iterations. This innovation unlocks new horizons in the avenue of materiobiology and biomedicine. The multifarious exploration of luminescent nanodots and their pliable association with versatile imaging techniques have made impressive strides in the biomedical realm, particularly in onco-theranostics. Enlarged nuclei indicate tumor proliferation, and these manifestations can be rendered visible with the aid of fluorescence (FL) imaging. Herein, we present the “cradle-to-gate” transformation of Mueller Hinton agar (MHA) into a biological probe with alluring physicochemical attributes. More importantly, a comprehensive understanding of how each parameter intricately shapes the properties of nanomaterials remains elusive. To bridge this gap, mathematically derived grey relational analysis (GRA) and a soft-computing-based modelling tool-artificial neural network (ANN) were proposed in this work to impose stringent control on the fabrication of ultra-small (∼3 nm) nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) with high quantum yield (QY). NCQDs demonstrated remarkable antijamming performance and high photostability, making them ideal for bioimaging. Moreover, the water-soluble luminous pearls served as a biocompatible trident in cancer biology, achieving a three-pronged action of in-vitro cell photostability, multi-color imaging, and migration without hampering the biological system. The histological and biochemical analysis demonstrated no overt toxicity of NCQDs in mice, even under multi-dosing situations. The convergence of experimental and simulation results vouch for the potential fabrication of a simple and powerful “diagnostic” nanoplatform for highly effective medical diagnosis. This breakthrough offers substantial biomedical applications in the future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the time of Galileo, imaging techniques have become the “eyes of science” driving substantial advancements in biomedical research and health diagnosis. This technique offers a wide range of information on various intricate biological processes due to the fast feedback and diffraction-limited spatial resolution [1,2]. The adaptation of light energy in medical science has sparked the emergence of a burgeoning area called photomedicine. Photomedicine has garnered significant attention due to its non-invasiveness, spatiotemporal controllability, and cytocompatible approach. The blooming application of light toward medicine requires photoactive agents. Numerous photoactive agents are catalogued by fundamental descriptions of their chemical identities, surface chemistry, quantitative photoluminescence (PL) properties, physical morphology, and PL mechanisms [3]. The emergence of several bioimaging modalities, such as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), computed axial tomography (CAT), photoacoustic imaging (PI), positron-emission tomography, ultrasound imaging, and fluorescence technique (FL), facilitates clear visualization of multi-dimensional data, simplifying disease diagnosis [1]. Among all modalities, the eminence of FL has attracted considerable attention for cancer diagnosis and image-guided therapy owing to its electrifying features, such as non-invasiveness, low photodamage, convenience, good penetration, low cost, reduced auto-fluorescence, and minimal detrimental effects [4]. However, conventional fluorophores such as organic dyes (e.g., cyanine dyes, rhodamine, and porphyrin) and nanoparticles (NPs; e.g., gold NPs, silicon NPs, and semiconducting quantum dots (SQDs)), encounter significant challenges including poor FL performance, low aqueous dispersibility, time-limited imaging, and toxicity, which effectively hinder their effectiveness. Therefore, the quest for benign nanomaterials with remarkable optical properties and improved pharmacokinetics continues [5].

CQDs (artificial atoms), a mystic star in the carbon family, have emerged as a potential substitute for conventional photosensitizers in photomedicine [6]. CQDs are quasi-0D and sp2/sp3 hybridized carbon-based fluorescent particles defined by a size < 10 nm [7]. They consist of a graphitic and turbostratic carbon core adorned with copious functional moieties (e.g., –OH, –COOH, and –NH2) on their surface. The presence of organogenic (e.g., Hydrogen, Carbon, Oxygen, and Nitrogen) elements and surface functional groups render hydrophilic properties and distinctive optoelectrical characteristics to the CQDs, enabling them as attractive tools for bio-imaging applications [3,8]. CQDs own distinctive features such as excellent PL, multi-color emission profile, large stokes shift, high photostability, sustainability, tunable surface functionalization, biocompatibility, and facile functionalization [9,10]. These merits make CQDs a very attractive and enticing modality for various domains, spanning from cellular imaging to targeted drug delivery [7,8].

The hetero-atom doping strategy efficiently modulates the intrinsic properties of CQDs. It can be categorized into single-doped and co-doped depending on the number of heteroatoms introduced into the carbon framework. The optoelectronic prospects of CQDs can be modulated actively by incorporating atomic impurities like Nitrogen (N), Sulphur (S), Boron (B), Phosphorus (P), and others into the CQD structure. Nitrogen doping improves the PL properties of CQDs. Nitrogen, as a dopant, possesses five valence electrons and an atomic size akin to carbon. To date, several bottom-up approaches have blossomed for the controlled synthesis of nitrogen-doped CQDs (NCQDs), including solvothermal, hydrothermal, chemical oxidation, pyrolysis, and microwave. Among all techniques, the hydrothermal strategy tends to be an influential and economical synthesis technology that produces large amounts of fluorescent NCQDs from various carbonaceous precursors. This method alters the properties of carbon sources (dehydration and carbonization), thereby enhancing the targeted biological applications and polychromatic emission characteristics of CQDs in diverse fields [11]. For instance, Lai et al. [12] reported the pristine synthesis of N-CQDs through hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and o-phenylenediamine and achieved a QY of 33.55 %. On similar grounds, Gracia and coworkers prepared N-CDs from Terminalia chebula and ethylene-di-amine (EDA) using a hydrothermal strategy, yielding a QY of 19.99 % [13].

The controlled fabrication of nanodots with desired diameter and biophysical attributes hinges on the precise modulation of different reaction parameters, such as temperature, precursor amount, volume of solvent, and time. The combinatorial influence of all synthesis parameters can be discerned through multi-dimensional optimization approaches [14]. Consequently, understanding the relationship between the process parameters and quality attributes will have a far-reaching influence on their diverse applications. In recent years, artificial intelligence (AI) has shown potential applications in the field of chemistry and biology, such as spectroscopic analysis, cancer biomarker detection, and biosensor advancement. Issa et al. [15] reported the combinatorial effects of reaction time (RT), hydrothermal temperature (HT), and EDA on the QY of N-CDs via central composite design-based response surface methodology (CCD-RSM). Likewise, Barati and coworkers [16] explored the CCD-RSM approach to illustrate the combinatorial significances of HT, RT, and NH4OH concentration upon QY of N-CQDs. In another study, Pudza and colleagues [17] reported the cumulative effects of HT, precursor dosage, RT, and solvent ratio on CDs’ QY efficiency via the RSM-ANN (artificial neural network) approach. However, achieving precise fabrication of an ideal nanoprobe for diverse high-flux applications such as diagnostics, biosensing, bioimaging, and drug delivery demands low invasiveness, swift detection, and superior performance.

The as-synthesized NCQDs were comprehensively characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), Dynamic light scattering (DLS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Ultraviolet–Visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis), and PL techniques. Detailed experimental protocols are discussed in the Supporting Information file. Moreover, details of the ninhydrin reaction and stability study of NCQDs under various environmental factors are elaborated in the Supporting Information file.

The biocompatible nature of NCQDs was probed by blood compatibility studies, in-vitro cell-culture studies (MTT, clonogenic assay, wound-healing assay, morphology assay, acridine orange/ethidium bromide (AO/EB) assay, and photostability) and in-vivo studies (ex-vivo imaging, haemanalysis, and biochemical analysis, detection of NCQDs in blood serum via scanning electron microscope coupled energy dispersive spectroscopy (SEM-EDS), and histocompatibility). Moreover, the in-vitro intracellular uptake study of NCQDs was conducted via a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) and flow cytometry. The Supporting Information file presents the detailed experimental procedure of these biomedical studies. The animal experimental model is a pre-requisite to evaluate the toxic effect of any foreign materials before the final clinical trial. Thus, to get the acute (short-term) toxic effect, the in-vivo toxicity was undertaken in Swiss albino mice model using three different experimental groups: Group 1 (Control; PBS, 100 μL), Group 2 (1 mg/kg of OE sample), and Group 3 (10 mg/kg of OE sample). The doses selected closely emulated the clinical conditions and were administered through i.v. route every other day for 7 and 14 days. Body weight, blood parameters, and histopathological studies were also undertaken to find any signs of toxicity and inflammation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of optimal synthesis condition through the GRA approach
ce:section.ce:para:
A robust inter-relationship between reference and comparative sequences is accomplished when the comparability sequence displays a higher magnitude of the grey grade [14]. Based on this hypothesis, the 7th experimental trial (A2, B3, C4, D1, and E2) was designated as the best experimental combination (see Table 2 ) as it shows the highest grey grade score among all experiments. Fig. 1
(a) graphically depicts the GRG corresponding to each experimental trial. GRG also has a provision for the confirmation of the optimized experiment through the generation of a response table ( Table S4 ) and graph ( Fig. 1
(b)). According to Table S4 and Fig. 1
(b), A2, B2, C4, D1, and E2 (HT=190 °C, RT=10 h, MMHA=400 mg, Vwater = 60 mL, VEDA=7 mL) were determined as the optimal input parameter sets. Therefore, it is imperative to conduct a confirmation test at A2, B2, C4, D1, and E2 and then compare the response characteristics of this combination relative to those obtained from the 7th experimental trial.

The confirmation test ( Table 3
) exhibited superior NCQD characteristics compared to the 7th experimental trial. This is attributed to the reduction in RT in the confirmation test. Lower RT primarily governs the carbonization efficiency during the HTC, resulting in the generation of small-sized NCQDs. Additionally, a shortened RT improves the nitrogen absorption efficiency of NCQDs, facilitating their uniform distribution marked by small size, negligible defects, and diminished propensity of agglomeration [20]. Conversely, prolonged carbonization yields NCQDs with larger dimensions. When the size of NCQDs surpasses the exciton Bohr radius, the quantum confinement effect is impaired, subsequently reducing the QY. These improvements mold the NCQDs as an ideal probe for future bioimaging applications. Conclusively, the A2, B2, C4, D1, and E2 parametric combination was inferred as the best-optimized experimental condition (OE), while the parametric combination corresponding to the 7th experiment (A2, B3, C4, D1, and E2) was labeled as the initial experimental condition (IE). Table S4
and
Fig. 1
(b) underscore the predominant effect of Vwater on all quality attributes of NCQDs due to the substantial difference between the maximum and minimum grey grade score (0.2598). Henceforth, Vwater emerged as a principal synthesis parameter, succeeded by MMHA, HT, VEDA, and RT on a multi-performance level. Water, as a solvent, exhibits higher polarization than organic solvents (ethanol and acetonitrile (ACN)). This characteristic marks an efficient HTC reaction, yielding an outstanding degree of carbonization and luminescence intensity [21,22]. Furthermore, Vwater plays a prime role in the dehydrolysis process, particularly during the amidation step. When Vwater falls below the optimal level, amidation occurs readily, resulting in the abundant formation of pyrrolic N species. On the contrary, surpassing the optimal level of Vwater hinders the amidation reaction. However, Vwater also governs the formation of COOH and C=O groups on the NCQDs’ surface [23]. Hence, achieving the optimal Vwater ensures a delicate equilibrium between the amidation and carboxylation processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simulation of datasets using ANN and additional verification experiment
ce:section.ce:para:
ANN serves as a prediction tool that correlates the process and response parameters through the use of self-defined weights and bias. A well-trained network can be effortlessly used in predicting the NCQDs performances for any intermediate synthesis parameters range. Herein, a 5-16-3 ANN architecture is proposed (see Fig. 1
(c)), which utilizes the NCQDs dataset from Table 2 for its training, testing, and validations. A detailed analysis of ANN is provided in the Supporting Information file. Based on the training datasets, the response characteristics are also predicted via the 5-16-3 ANN model, as summarized in Table 4
. Moreover, the error between experimental and predicted NCQD characteristics has been evaluated and presented in Table 4 . Here, a close trade-off between the experimental and ANN-generated response characteristics has been noticed, indicating a perfectly trained and validated model. Moreover, the 5-16-3 model exhibits a mean square error (MSE) of 3.21e-27 at 290 iterations, further pointing to the model’s effectiveness while predicting the NCQDs response attributes. Subsequently, the regression score (R2) of one (∼1) (from Fig. 1
(d)) signifies a perfectly trained and validated 5-16-3 feed-forward ANN architecture ( Fig. 1
(d)).

A high QY serves as an essential photophysical parameter for multiplexed cellular imaging [7]. The ANOVA analysis ( Table S6 ) and % contribution graph ( Fig. S1 ) revealed that HT and VEDA are the most influential variables affecting the QY of NCQDs. The effect of various synthesis conditions on the QY of NCQDs is framed using a matrix plot ( Fig. 2
(a)). Moreover, the 3D surface graph ( Fig. 2 (b)) outlines the impact of HT and VEDA over QY.

HT significantly regulates the degree of carbonization during the synthesis process. HT develops a controlled condition for the formation of carbon core. At 170 °C, insufficient carbonization restricts the formation of carbon core, leading to a lower QY. When the HT exceeds 170 °C, the nucleation and carbonization process becomes more dominant, strengthening the carbon core. Furthermore, elevating the HT above 220 °C may induce over-carbonization and by-product formation, consequently diminishing the QY [24,25]. Excessive HT results in broad-size distributions and numerous surface defects and can lead to a total or partial loss of surface functional groups of NCQDs. These effects collectively impair the spectral properties of QDs and quench their FL intensity [26]. Also, as the HT rises, molecules move faster and collide more frequently per unit time due to higher average kinetic energy. This heightened molecular motion obstructs the formation of stable NCQDs. Conversely, lower HT impedes the fabrication of small and stable NCQDs by limiting the energy required for essential chemical transformations. Hence, the precise optimization of HT is paramount during the synthesis of NCQDs.

Meanwhile, QY of NCQDs shares a negative relationship with the VEDA. The addition of EDA plays a pivotal role in controlling the amination process during the hydrothermal fabrication of NCQDs, consequently affecting both the carbonization rate and QY. In this work, EDA significantly boosts the efficiency of the reaction process by serving as both an alkaline catalyst and an active reactant. The N-atom present in the EDA injects electrons into the framework of CQDs and modifies their internal electronic environment. This modification leads to an enhancement in their PL properties [27,28]. As an organic passivator, EDA mitigates the formation of structural defects and introduces disorder in the hexagonal ring structure of the CQDs.

Additionally, it facilitates the creation of emissive trap states within the crystalline sp2 core [29,30]. These trap states contribute towards higher FL and QY of NCQDs. Fig. 2
(a-b) demonstrates that the QY of as-synthesized NCQDs remains consistently high when VEDA falls within a range of 5 to 7 mL, regardless of the MMHA added. This observation underscores that including EDA within this range efficiently promotes the HTC reaction. Moreover, when the VEDA exceeds 7–11 mL, the QY of the as-synthesized NCQDs exhibits a decreasing pattern. This observation aligns harmoniously with the rule that an extreme infusion of EDA (beyond the optimized level) transitions its function from promoting the reaction to passivating the surface of the NCQDs, thereby impeding the overall reaction rate [31,32]. In the present study, a high QY of 40.31 % is attained under the OE experimental condition, demonstrating the feasibility and effectiveness of GRA-coupled ANN-guided synthesis.

The size distribution of nanomaterials is usually defined by the PDI. It signifies the heterogeneity of particle sizes within the tested sample [33]. According to Mudalige et al. [34], a sample is said to be monodispersed (<0.05) or polydispersed (>0.7) based on their PDI values. The formation of agglomerates impedes interactions between biological tissues and nanomaterials due to a decrease in mass-specific surface area with increasing particle size. Therefore, the synthesis of monodispersed nanomaterials is crucial for diverse biomedical applications.

Vwater and MMHA are the prime reaction factors that have maximum regulation over the PDI of as-synthesized NCQDs, followed by RT, VEDA, and HT. The effect of diverse reaction parameters on PDI is graphically portrayed in the form of a matrix plot ( Fig. 2
(c)). The significance of Vwater and MMHA on PDI is established via a 3D surface graph ( Fig. 2
(d)). Water in the HTC process primarily acts as a solvent and a chemical component for the hydrolysis reaction [35]. The Vwater in the reaction mixture affects the kinetics, mechanism of NP formation, and nucleation process. From Fig. 2
(c-d), it can be inferred that PDI and Vwater share a positive correlation during the HTC process. In other words, the PDI of the NCQDs increases with higher Vwater. This positive correlation can be explained by the two-fold role played by water. Higher Vwater accelerates the rate of hydrolysis, which subsequently promotes the rapid generation of reactive species and a faster rate of nucleation [36]. This phenomenon may result in broader particle size distribution (PSD) and higher PDI. Moreover, at higher HT and Vwater, molecules gain more kinetic energy, leading to enhanced aggregation and collision events. As a result, a broader distribution of particle sizes and a higher PDI occurs [37,38]. Notably, exceeding the optimal Vwater level hinders the graphitization process during the HTC reaction by occupying surface sites on the carbon precursors or interacting with them [23].

In addition, an inverse trend is observed between MMHA and PDI, i.e., the PSD of the NCQDs becomes narrower (more uniform size distribution) with an increase in the MMHA. Higher MMHA provides an ample supply of reactants or reactive species, facilitating homogenous and controlled growth of QDs. This promotes consistent nucleation and growth rates, reduces the likelihood of Ostwald ripening and aggregation, and maintains stable reaction kinetics. Pimpang et al. [39] illustrated that large precursor molecules undergo complete breakdown into nano-sized particles under high HTs. The nano-sized particles further signify low PDI with enhanced PL intensity and QY [40]. A low PDI is associated with high QY owing to consistent optical properties and fewer surface defects. In the realm of bioimaging, low PDI of diagnostic agents provides brighter images with enhanced photostability.

CO reflects the ability of the NCQDs to facilitate fast charge transportation, generation, and emission of intense luminescent signals. MMHA and HT vary monotonically with CO (as observed in Fig. 2
(e-f)). Despite the significant presence of MMHA, ultra-small NCQDs were fabricated due to complete carbonization under enhanced HT conditions. The ultra-small size of NCQDs bolsters the available surface area for electrochemical reactions. Moreover, the increased surface area allows more active sites for charge transfer, leading to high current density, high capacity retention, and fast charge transportation owing to elevated CO [41]. Under high CO, the rapid movement of charged particles facilitates the development of brighter and more stable FL signals, thereby indicating a higher QY. Higher CO also enhances the photostable attribute of NCQDs by promoting more efficient energy transfer processes. High CO enhances QY via reducing non-radiative pathways and minimizes defects. Enhanced CO allows the effective traverse of NCQDs into the targeted cells or tissues, facilitating comprehensive analysis and diagnosis. Wonder electrical CO allows stable interaction of NCQDs with the biomolecules (antibodies, peptides, or nucleic acids), thus acting as ideal fluorescent biological probes [42]. Moreover, the CO of NCQDs increases with decreasing particle size because of the large surface area-to-volume ratio [43]. Therefore, optimizing the CO of diagnostic agents is of paramount importance for maximizing their effectiveness in bioimaging applications.

The morphological characterization of the NCQDs synthesized under both IE and OE conditions was carried out by TEM ( Fig. 3
(a-h)). Fig. 3 unveiled the quasi-spherical structure of both IE ( Fig. 3
(a)) and OE ( Fig. 3
(e)) samples with a homogenous size distribution. The monodisperse property of spherical NCQDs was achieved through the uniform heating of MHA during the HTC. The size distribution histogram obeys a Gaussian distribution, reflecting the nucleation of about 9.25 nm and 3.12 nm for IE ( Fig. 3
(b)) and OE ( Fig. 3
(f)) samples, respectively. The size of the NCQDs increases from 3.12 nm to 9.25 nm with increasing RT. Moreover, a shorter RT improves the nitrogen absorption efficiency of NCQDs, resulting in their uniform distribution, small size, negligible defects, and reduced chances of agglomeration [20]. The ultra-small size of the OE sample allows them to traverse the gaps (4–6 nm) between endothelial cells of the blood–brain barrier (BBB), thereby facilitating the exertion of the pharmacological function [44]. The HR-TEM images of IE ( Fig. 3
(c)) and OE ( Fig. 3
(g)) samples revealed a well-resolved crystal lattice with an interplanar spacing of ∼ 2.2 Å, which is consistent with the {100} diffraction facet of sp2 graphitic carbon [29,45]. In corroboration, the broad diffused rings detected in the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns reflect the poly-nanocrystalline nature of archetypal NCQDs ( Fig. 3
(d(IE) & h(OE))) [13]. The absence of bright spots in the SAED patterns indicates that the domains are devoid of rotational stacking faults, akin to turbostratic graphite [46]. The NCQDs were further probed by dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique, as discussed in the Supporting Information file ( Fig. S2 ).

Powder XRD was employed to identify the crystallographic structure and phase purity of the NCQDs (IE and OE). Both IE and OE samples exhibit similar crystal structures with broad diffraction peaks. As displayed in Fig. 3
(i), both IE and OE samples show a broad halo at 2θ≈24°, pointing to the amorphous nature of as-synthesized NCQDs [47,48]. The broadening of the {002} peak is ascribed to the nanoregime NCQDs and imperfect graphitization [49]. A slight shift in the amorphous halo of sample IE might be due to the increase in particle size.

The formation mechanism and the chemical composition of NCQDs were examined by FTIR spectra and XPS. The FTIR spectra ( Fig. 3
(j)) of the MHA display the presence of C–O (1036 cm−1), C=O (1638 cm−1), and O–H (2800 cm−1 to 3600 cm−1) stretching vibrations, indicating the existence of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. In addition, the characteristic vibration centered at 1595 cm−1 corresponds to the N–H bending vibration of EDA. The weak shoulder peaks at 1168 cm−1 and 1055 cm−1 originated from C–N stretching vibrations. The amine groups of EDA, in combination with the hydroxyl/carboxyl groups of MHA, facilitate subsequent dehydration, condensation, and carbonization, resulting in the pristine formation of NCQDs [50].

Notably, IE and OE samples possess similar bond-level vibrations. The ensemble of vicinal peaks related to intermolecular interactions via H-bridges (O–H) is evidenced by a broad absorption peak around 3000–3500 cm−1. Post HTC, the O–H stretching peak becomes broader and splits into two distinct regions: 3100–3300 cm−1 and 3300–3500 cm−1. According to bibliography [51], these regions reflect the symmetric and anti-symmetric stretching vibrations of the N–H group, respectively. The peaks at 2930 cm−1 to 2877 cm−1 emanated in response to the C–H (–CH2) stretch of sp3 and sp2 hybridized carbons in alkanes and alkenes, respectively [52,53]. The conspicuous sharp peak at 2300 cm−1 can be ascribed to the C≡N (sp1) bond stretching, which is consistent with the previous reports [51,54]. The oxide-related peak at 1645 cm−1 can be attributed to the amide carbonyl (C=O) stretching for both IE and OE samples [51,55–58]. The in-plane bending vibration of N-H (amide) at 1560 cm−1 (originated from EDA) suggests the existence of a secondary amine [56,58,59]. Meanwhile, the C═C aromatic ring stretch is noticed at 1544 cm−1 [31,56,59]. The peak at 1432 cm−1 is mainly attributed to C–N=stretching vibration [59,60]. The peak at 1389 cm−1 is accredited to the asymmetric bending of CH2 in the NCQDs [51,56]. Besides, the peak at 1272 cm−1
[31,55,59] corresponds to C–O vibrations, while 1315 cm−1 represents stretching vibrations of C–N heterocycles [51,55,57]. The C–O stretching vibration of epoxy is indexed at 1038 cm−1. Notably, the decline in the band intensity of the oxygen functionalities (C–O, C=O) in the OE sample reflects the deoxygenation reaction caused by the reducing effect of EDA.

The XPS survey spectrum of the NQCDs ( Fig. 4
) highlights the presence of three dominant energy peaks: C1s (Carbon; 284.8 eV), N1s (Nitrogen; 398.1 eV), and O1 s (Oxygen; 531 eV) [31]. Table S7 summarizes the atomic % of C1s, N1s, and O1s in NCQDs. The C1s spectrum ( Fig. 4
(b)) consists of four prominent peaks: namely C−C/C=C bonds (sp2 carbons), C−O/C−N bonds (sp3 carbons), C=O/C=N bonds (carbonyl carbons), and COOH bonds (carboxyl carbons). For the IE sample, these peaks are observed at 284.6, 285.7, 287.0 and 287.9 eV. For the OE sample, the peaks are cognized at 284.3, 285.2, 286.2, and 287.4 eV [52]. The deconvoluted N1s spectrum ( Fig. 4
(c)) shows three Gaussian peaks: pyridinic N, amino N, and pyrrolic N at 398.0, 397.5, and 398.8 eV for IE samples, and at 398.2, 397.6, and 399 eV for OE samples, respectively [61]. These N-bonding peaks unequivocally affirm the effective incorporation of “N” in the basal plane network structure of NCQDs [31]. The pyridinic N is predominantly present within the core region, while amino N and graphitic N are identified at the edge sites. The deconvoluted O1s spectrum shows two signals: C=O (carbonyl group) and C−O (epoxy group) at 530.5 and 531.2 eV for the IE sample and at 530.8 and 531.9 eV for the OE sample, respectively [62]. The percent proportions of the aforementioned “C” and “O” moieties are displayed in Table S8 and S9 , respectively. The atomic N/C ratio (RN/C) of 16.37/72.32 and 17.82/68.82 for the IE and OE samples, respectively, underscores the prevalence of “C” as the primary element. The RN/C % of the as-synthesized OE sample (∼26 %) is significantly higher compared to NCQDs synthesized by Koc et al. [32] using the HTC route. The decline in the RO/C from the IE to the OE sample is attributed to the expulsion of the water molecules following the condensation of carboxylic acid and amino groups [52]. The N-doping strategy elevates the QY of NCQDs. Incorporation of N-dopants into the framework of CQDs reduces the occurrence of non-radiative sites, thereby promoting the formation of C=O bonds. These C=O bonds, in turn, enhance the QY of NCQDs by promoting radiation recombination processes [31].

The existence of amino acid groups on the edge of the OE sample was investigated using the classical ninhydrin reaction. The amino acid-ninhydrin (strong electrophilic reagent) reaction yields a purple-colored compound (diketohydrindylidene diketohydrindamine), often referred to as Ruhemann’s purple (RP). This distinctive purple-colored product displays a prominent absorption peak at 570 nm. The presence of amino acid groups on the OE sample can be probed by measuring the absorbance of RP. The emergence of a new adsorption peak at 570 nm (brown line) was evidenced upon reacting the OE sample (yellow line) with the ninhydrin reagent, as pictured in Fig. 5
(a). This observation signifies the positive presence of amino acid moieties on the edge of as-synthesized NCQDs [50].

UV–Vis absorption and PL spectroscopic analysis were deeply explored to assess the photophysical characteristics of NCQDs. The UV–Vis spectrum exhibited three absorption peaks at 225 nm, 290 nm, and 333 nm for the OE sample ( Fig. 5
(b)) and 232 nm, 290 nm, and 333 nm for the IE sample ( Fig. 5
(c)) with a tail spanning into the visible region of the spectrum. The short wavelength bands at 225 nm (OE sample) and 232 nm (IE sample) represent the π-π* transition of the aromatic C=C bond, generating no FL signal [59,63]. The absorption shoulder at 333 nm is ascribed to the n-π* transition associated with the C=O bond [13,51]. Another peak at 290 nm (C=N) reflects the characteristic transition due to the successful doping of N-atoms at the edges and defects of NCQDs. The broad tail presumably signifies the existence of different functional groups on the surface of NCQDs [64]. Specifically, the broad absorbance peak of the IE sample reflects the less monodispersed nature of the sample as compared to the OE sample [65]. The absorption peak at 225 nm is red-shifted in the IE sample due to the more significant effect, unveiling a larger π-π* conjugated domain [62]. Notably, shortening the RT improved the quality of the OE sample, evidenced by a decrease of C=N bonds and a concomitant augmentation of C=O bonds [23]. The ultra-small size of NCQDs, along with their restricted sp2 domains, leads to negligible absorption of light within the visible spectrum. Consequently, they appear as yellowish hue when dispersed in a solution [66]. More intuitively, the inset of Fig. 5
(b) and (c) represent the digital photographs of the aqueous suspension of IE and OE samples under UV lamp irradiation (365 nm). Both samples emitted persistent and reproducible blue FL under UV irradiation while appearing transparent pale-yellow under sunlight due to their lower electron density. In contrast, the aqueous solution of MHA ( Fig. S3
(a)) and EDA ( Fig. S3
(b)) are colorless under ambient daylight with negligible PL emission under UV irradiation, indicating the formation of quantum-sized NCQDs after the HTC. The direct band gap (Eg) of IE ( Fig. S4
(a)) and OE ( Fig. S4
(b)) samples was approximated to be 2.94 eV and 3.19 eV, respectively, as determined from the Tauc Equation (E Q s. S1) [14,67,68].

The PL spectrum displays the λex-dependent emission FL behavior for both IE ( Fig. 5
(d)) and OE ( Fig. 5
(g)) samples with FL maxima ranging from 300 to 500 nm with a step length of 20 nm. The internal surface defect states, uneven heterogeneity of size, and the molecular FL of NCQDs serve as the dominant factors for λex-dependent emission characteristics [13,60]. The λex-dependent emission characteristics support the multi-color imaging attribute of NCQDs, as shown in Figs. S5
(IE) and S6
(OE). For both the samples (IE and OE), the PL emission intensity initially rises from 300 to 380 nm due to electron transition from n to the π* orbital of the graphitic bond cores, then bathochromically shifts from 400-500 nm with a gradual decrease in intensity due to low optical absorption in the Vis region [69]. Moreover, the samples displayed the maximum FL peak and photoluminescence excitation wavelength (PLE) at around 464 nm and 380 nm, respectively, as they share the exact luminescence mechanism and emitting centers [23]. The full width at half-maximum (FWHM) of the PL spectrum for NCQDs (IE and OE samples) is about ∼ 111 nm, which is a clear signature for a narrow spectral profile owing to the high-degree graphitization and crystallinity [31]. In addition, the stokes shift value exhibited a notable decrease from 144 nm to 45 nm as the λex shifted from 300 to 500 nm ( Table S10 ). The significant stokes shift stands as a highly desirable quality for FL imaging. Fig. 5
(e) & (h) display the normalized PL graph of IE and OE samples. The excitation-emission map, depicted in Fig. 5
(f (IE) & I (OE)), illustrates blue as the primary fluorescent color. The absolute PLQY values of NCQDs under IE and OE experimental conditions were calculated to be 34 % and 40.31 %, respectively. Interestingly, the higher intensity of the emission spectra in the OE sample reflects higher QY and RN/C. In addition, we can observe an inverse relationship between the QY and particle size of NCQDs. This response can be ascribed to the variations in the density of surface emissive trap sites, influenced by the number of photoexcitable electrons [21].

The stability and the antijamming performance of bioprobes under various conditions were characterized in view of the practical applications. The temporal stability of NCQDs is a crucial issue, as their uncertain degradation potential could result in serious side effects and unstable imaging outcomes due to the oxidation process [70]. We first assessed the temporal stability of NCQDs in water by monitoring the changes in their size, PDI, FL emission, and absorbance spectra as a function of different shelf times. We found that the diameter and PDI of NCQDs did not change over 12 days after incubation in water, demonstrating excellent colloidal stability ( Fig. S7
(a)). QDs form aggregates at higher concentrations, while at lower concentrations, individual dots are enveloped by a solvation shell. The shell is a consequence of the static charge (COO–) present on their surface, rendering them non-toxic for cellular uptake. In addition, the absorbance spectra ( Fig. S7
(b)) and the FL emission intensity ( Fig. S7
(c)) of NCQDs in aqueous solution did not change over 12 days and retained more than 98 % of their initial values.

We further characterized the stability of NCQDs under various biologically relevant pH conditions, ranging from 1 to 13 (pH ≈ 1.5 in gastric juice; pH ≈ 4.5–5 in lysosomes; pH ≈ 5.5–6 in cellular compartments, including endosomes; pH ≈ 6–7 inside tumors and inflamed tissues; pH ≈ 8.5 in the small intestine). Our findings revealed that the PL intensity of NCQDs exhibited slight decay in severe acidic and basic environments ( Fig. S7
(e)) while remained unchanged in all other tested conditions. This performance is described as the protonation-deprotonation of their surface functional groups under different pH values. Specifically, at lower pH values, increased protonation leads to non-reductive recombination of excited electrons. In contrast, high pH induces the accumulation of a negatively charged shell on the NCQDs’ surface, resulting in PL quenching [71–73].

Moreover, NCQDs solution demonstrated excellent antijamming performance even under high ionic strength environments (up to 1.50 M NaCl) ( Fig. S7
(f)). The NCQDs solution maintained a long-term homogenous phase without any visible precipitation owing to the minimal ionization of functional groups present on their surface [71].

In the current era of nanomedicine, rapid pre-clinical screening of nanoprobes is essential for combating a wide array of diseases. Prior to the exploration of NPs in-vivo applications, the first-line toxicity test, “haemolysis assay”, offers a rapid and high-throughput method for screening the material’s biocompatibility. The haemolysis assay works by detecting the leakage of intracellular haemoglobin (Hb) into the blood plasma, owing to the impairment of RBC’s intact membrane. Nanodots can compromise RBC function and membrane integrity by generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) or mechanical damage. In addition, the haemolytic properties of dots are significantly influenced by their physicochemical attributes, including surface charge, particle size, surface functionalization, morphology, and surface roughness [74,75]. Fig. 6
(a, b) shows the haemolytic profile of NCQDs in a dose-dependent manner (50–500 μg/mL). No discernible differences were observed between the negative control (NC) and the test groups, even at the highest tested concentration throughout the entire time window of 1h. The haemolytic ratio of our as-synthesized NCQDs was < 5 % across the board. As per the ASTM E2524-08 standard, the haemolytic grade can be classified into three levels: >5% haemolysis (haemolytic), 2–5 % (slightly haemolytic), and < 2 % (haemocompatible) [76].

Sheer stress size, degree of the electrostatic repulsion, and macromolecular bridge force majorly affect the aggregability of erythrocytes [14]. Under physiological conditions, mammalian RBCs exhibit a biconcave-disk (discocyte) shape, facilitating easy aggregation owing to their larger contact area. Conversely, deformed RBCs with oval or spherical shape experience weakened aggregation due to their smaller contact area [77]. As anticipated, NCQDs did not restrict the structural flexibility and integrity of the RBCs membrane, indicating no RBC deformability (haemagglutination inhibition). The visual representation of haeamgglutination assay is exhibited in triplicates ( Fig. 6
(c)). In essence, haemolysis and the haemagglutination assays demonstrate that particle size does not overturn the haemocompatibility of conductive NCQDs [14,77,78].

The biconcave discoid-shaped RBCs of healthy mammals may undergo structural deformability upon interaction with NPs [79]. Having validated the haemocompatible profile of NCQDs via the in-vitro haemolysis and haemagglutination assays, we further turned to acquire images of RBC morphology for better evaluation of their biocompatibility at 500 μg/mL. Fig. 6
(e-g) represents the RBCs morphology after their association with IE ( Fig. 6
(f)) and OE ( Fig. 6
(g)) samples. Visual inspection under the microscope revealed the intact morphology of RBCs with no sign of morphological alterations after being treated with NCQDs. The negative surface charge of NCQDs reduced the electrostatic interactions with the negatively charged components of the RBC membrane, further safeguarding the RBC’s structural integrity. Moreover, the surface functional groups could facilitate the formation of a hydration shell around the NCQDs, thereby preventing direct contact with the RBC membrane and thus mitigating the chances of mechanical damage. The RBCs morphology in the NC group ( Fig. 6
(e)) is very similar to the test groups.

The probable effect of NCQDs on the secondary structure of plasma is displayed in Fig. 6
(d). Notably, the CD spectra of the plasma exhibit characteristic negative bands at 208 (π–π* transition) and 222 nm (n–π* transition), demonstrating an α-helical property [80,81]. After interaction with NCQDs, no deviation in the peak position was observed, representing no alteration in the secondary structures of plasma proteins. The % ellipticity of α-helix and β-sheet was calculated using the K2D3 tool. The relative % was computed to be α-25.7 %, β-15.7 % (pristine plasma protein); α-26.2 %, β-16.7 % (OE sample); α-25.2 %, β-16.8 % (IE sample). The positive adsorption of plasma proteins on the NCQDs’ surface did not induce any loss of α-helix conformation.

The MTT (3-[4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl]-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay is a straightforward, cost-effective, and rapid colorimetric technique that evaluates mitochondrial dehydrogenase activity to gauge the cell viability. This method relies on the principle that only metabolically active cells (viable cells) with functional mitochondria can convert yellow color MTT reagent to purple formazan crystals, serving as the quantifiable end product of this assay. As NCQDs are intended for biomedical applications, the cytotoxic potential of NCQDs against LN229 cells was investigated using MTT assay [76]. Here, a concentration range from 6.25-400 µg/mL was considered, where each experimental trail was repeated thrice (n = 3), and their average values along with standard deviation is pictured in Fig. 7
(a). Neither IE nor OE samples affected the proliferation rate of LN229 cells. Remarkably, both samples demonstrated more than 95 % survival even after treatment with high NCQDs concentration (400 μg mL−1). While comparing with control data (no NCQDs treatment), the treated cells showed insignificant variation in their viability. For further studies, the OE sample was selected owing to its exceptional QY, low PDI, and high CO, and it was renamed as “NCQDs”.

The clonogenic or survival assay serves as a standard in-vitro cell sensitivity experiment aimed at evaluating the long-term effects of therapeutic drugs and diagnostic agents on the proliferative ability of cells, i.e., the ability of a single cell to grow into a colony [82]. A cluster of at least 50 or more cells is defined as a colony. However, by assessing the ability of single cells to form colonies, researchers can gain deep insights into the cytostatic and cytotoxic effects of treatments, contributing to the understanding of cellular responses and the innovative development of diagnostic strategies. As demonstrated in Fig. 7
(b) and (c), the survival fraction in the control group closely resembles the different concentrations of NCQDs, thus affirming the biocompatible attribute of NCQDs. This observation is requisite for theranostic approaches [83].

A wound healing assay (also known as scratch assay) was conducted to investigate the effect of NCQDs on the migratory potential of LN229 cells. As observed in
Fig. 7
(d) and (e), NCQDs stimulated the growth of glioma cells in a dose-dependent manner following a 24-h incubation period. The enhanced migratory potential of LN229 cells can be attributed to the presence of amino acids on the surface of NCQDs, as represented in Fig. 5
(a). Interestingly, the migration efficiency of the group treated with the highest concentration of NCQDs (400 μg/mL) closely resembled that of the control group. Moreover, the impact of NCQDs upon the morphology of LN229 cells was studied by morphological assessment assay using a phase-contrast microscope, as discussed in the Supporting Information file ( Fig. S10 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Apoptosis assay by acridine orange/ethidium bromide (AO/EB) staining
ce:section.ce:para:
The characteristic cytomorphological and biochemical hallmarks of apoptotic cells include cell shrinkage, blebbing, pyknosis (chromatin condensation), and karyorrhexis (nuclear fragmentation). These distinct features can be visualized by dual AO/EB staining. AO/EB was utilized to distinguish among the viable, apoptotic, and necrotic cells after successive treatment of LN229 cells with NCQDs [84]. The AO dye stains the nuclei of both viable and dead cells, while EB easily diffuses across the apoptotic and membrane compromised cells (leading to necrosis). When only AO penetrates the cell membrane, it emits green FL upon binding to DNA. In striking contrast, when EB penetrates inside the membrane of damaged or dead cells, it prevails over AO and results in the cell fluorescing red. Late apoptotic cells exhibit orange chromatin with condensed or often fragmented nuclei. Necrotic cells display bright orange FL with no condensed chromatin. Our findings ( Fig. 7
(f)) revealed that LN229 cells treated with various concentrations (0, 25, 50, 100, 200, and 400 μg/mL) of NCQDs showed no sign of apoptosis or necrosis, as evidenced by consistent green FL and intact cellular morphology.
The potential applications of the NCQDs as cellular probes were investigated via CLSM and flow cytometry studies.

The LN229 cells were incubated with NCQDs (400 μg/mL) at 37 °C for 24 h, and then their luminescence images were captured using CLSM modes [85]. As displayed in Fig. 7
(g), the photostable dots pierced into the cells and illuminated intense blue (408 nm), green (488 nm), yellow (514), and red (561 nm) FL under CLSM observation. The uniform distribution of FL throughout the internal structure of cells evidences the effective internalization of NCQDs into cancer cells, indicating the ability of the dots to traverse the plasma membrane and emit bright FL signals from the whole cytosolic region. Multi-color emissions in the nuclear region indicate active translocation of dots through nuclear pore complexes (NPCs). The size of the nuclear pores is ∼ 9 nm, so the OE sample (3 nm) can easily penetrate the nucleus [86]. This active translocation may be associated with an increased presence of ribosomes within both the cytoplasmic and nuclear regions of tumor cells. The untreated cells exhibited no FL owing to the absence of NCQDs. Hence, the framed confocal micrographs highlight NCQDs’ bio-labelling attribute, which is favorable for real-time optical cellular imaging as a consequence of caveolae-mediated endocytosis [87].

To further confirm the cellular uptake of NCQDs within the LN229 cells, the cells were fixed using 4 % PFA and subsequently permeabilized, as detailed in the Supporting Information file. Fig. S11 displays a series of time-lapse CLSM images. The culture media was replaced by the NCQDs’ solution (400 μg/mL) just 2 min prior to the commencement of the experiment. Notably, it was evident that the “ultrafast” dots penetrated the cell boundaries, followed by progressive accumulation within the cytoplasm, and ultimately localized within the nucleus region. NCQDs showed nucleus imaging at 6 min, and the whole cellular uptake process was accomplished within 48 min, which underlies the application of NCQDs for cell labelling and staining. This observation underscores that cellular uptake is a rapid and continuous process. Moreover, the FL intensity of glioma cells was obviously enhanced along with increased incubation time, signifying that the endocytic uptake of dots occurred in a time-dependent and sustained manner through passive transportation [88].

Eventually, to unequivocally confirm the precise location of the pristine dots within the cellular environment, images were recorded with a 3D Z-stack in CLSM [89]. The Z-stacked images, displayed on the left side of Fig. S12
, show the spatial arrangement of each slice at different vertical depths along the Z-axis of prestained LN229 cells. As shown in Fig. S12 , the FL intensity exhibited a discernible upsurge when transitioning from the cell’s top (z = 0 μm) and decreased as the focal plane reached the bottom (z=21 μm). This empirical observation accordantly affirms the endocytosis-driven internalization of dots by the cells into the cytosol and nucleus region, rather than superficially adsorbed or attached on the outer surface of the LN229 cells ( Fig. S13 ). Moreover, expressive blue signals in the Z-6 and Z-9 mirrors depict successful in-vitro bioimaging. A video illustrating this z-scanning profile has been recorded and incorporated in the Supporting Information file ( Video S1 ).

The cellular internalization of NCQDs by LN229 cells was quantitatively analyzed using the flow cytometric analysis at 488 nm laser excitation ( Fig. 8
(a) and (b)). Quantification analysis showed a substantial increase in the FL intensity of glioma cells upon increasing the concentration gradient of NCQDs. Approximately, NCQDs (400 µg/mL) treated LN229 cells displayed FL intensity seven times higher than that of pristine QDs under the FL1 channel. Similar outcomes were observed for the FL2 channel, where prestained cells exhibited five times high FL at 400 µg/mL. Notably, even under the same λex (488 nm), the FL signals of both FITC-and Phycoerythrin (PE), emitting at 515–545 nm and 564–606 nm, respectively, were distinctively detectable [85].

High photostability is a highly desired attribute of bioprobes for super-resolution and long-term FL imaging and image-guided surgery. Photobleaching poses a significant challenge during microscopic observations, affecting a wide range of traditional organic fluorophores and consequently undermining the quality of bioimaging. As per the pieces of literature [90], researchers enhance the photostability of organic dyes using covalently linked “protective” agents and antifading additives. The intracellular environments also affect the photostability of dyes. In this regard, we investigated the anti-photobleaching characteristics of NCQDs in LN229 cells for long-term fluorescent imaging. Herein, FITC was used as a control dye. As evidenced by Fig. 9
, NCQDs ( Fig. 9
(a-h)) exhibited excellent FL with negligible bleaching and sustained intracellular signals even after multiple laser scans, while the FL of the control dye ( Fig. 9
(i-p)) rapidly diminished. The photostable attribute of NCQDs can be attributed to the larger stokes shift. After 60 min laser irradiation, the FL intensity of NCQDs remained unchanged compared to the 0 min, while FITC lost 99 % of FL signals. A video depicting the photostability of NCQDs ( Video S2 ) and FITC ( Video S3 ) has been recorded and incorporated in the Supporting Information file.

The high stability of bioprobes in body fluids is pivotal for their subsequent in-vivo applications. After 10 min of i.v. administration of NCQDs, the Swiss Albino Mice were sacrificed, and major organs like the liver, kidney, spleen, and brain were collected for ex-vivo imaging via CLSM. Fig. 10
shows multi-color FL imaging of major organs under different λex. As evidenced by Fig. 10 , the NCQDs distributed in the major organs within a mere 10 min of i.v. administration, showcasing their ultrafast imaging attribute. NCQDs traversed the organ epithelial layer through the nano-leaking process. The brighter FL in the liver was due to the non-specific accumulation of NCQDs within hepatic tissues. More importantly, in contrast to the control group (PBS), the brain displayed much stronger FL intensity, providing clear evidence of the non-specific distribution and BBB-penetrating capability of the NCQDs in the brain [44]. BBB is the biological barrier that impedes the diffusion and transportation of pharmaceuticals from the bloodstream into the central nervous system (CNS). Given that the size of the NCQDs is ∼ 3 nm, they can easily traverse the gap between endothelial cells of the BBB [56]. Notably, our as-synthesized nanodots efficiently traversed the BBB for comprehensive brain imaging, even in the absence of conjugation with specific cell surface receptors and transport proteins such as transferrin and apolipoprotein. Li et al. [91] successfully synthesized transferrin-conjugated C-dots, which can cross the BBB through transferrin-mediated endocytosis, a process that is impeded for bare C-dots.

Toxicological assessment of biomaterials serves as a strong foundation to evaluate their long-term biotoxicity in animal models. Hence, to delve deeper into the biosafety profile of NCQDs, a comprehensive exploration of in-vivo toxicity was undertaken through a multiple-dosing regimen that closely emulated clinical drug administration. As the size of NCQDs is very similar to that of small proteins and viruses, they may cause an inflammatory response, affecting haematological factors, such as RBCs and WBCs, etc. Hence, in-vivo toxicity of the as-synthesized blue emissive nanodots was examined on healthy mice model via hemanalysis and biochemical analysis. At the end of 7 and 14 days, the blood samples were acquired from the Swiss Albino mice via cardiac puncture. Both the control and treated groups displayed a stable trend in the body weight (BW) curve ( Fig. 11
(a)), which warrants insignificant biotoxicity of the diagnostic system [92]. The levels of critical hematology indicators ( Fig. 11
(b-d)), such as HGB%, TRBCs, and TWBCs, exhibited no drastic difference between control and treated groups. Hence, hemanalysis indicated the negligible influence of pristine NCQDs on the blood routine indexes.

Besides the renal function indicator, creatinine level did not result in apparent fluctuation, similar to the PBS treatment group ( Fig. 11
(e)). Specifically, optimal urea levels in the bloodstream serve as reliable indicators of the overall health status of both the renal and hepatic systems. Higher levels of urea in the blood unveil the potential impairment of renal function, whereas lower levels of urea reflect damage or malnutrition of the liver. The hepatic indicators ( Fig. 11
(f-j)), such as aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alanine transaminase (ALT), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), total protein (TP), total bilirubin (TB), and albumin (ALB) were measured periodically. The values of hepatic indicators treated with NCQDs showed partial fluctuations compared to the control group (PBS), yet they remained within the normal range despite the significant accumulation of NCQDs in the liver. In addition, levels of triglycerides (TG) ( Fig. 11
(k)) were also within the normal range. After weeks of close examination, no abnormal behaviour was observed in the dot-labelled animal. In general, hemanalysis and biochemical analysis reflected the superior therapeutic efficiency of NCQDs, highlighting their significant promise for potential inclusion in future clinical trials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of NCQDs in blood serum
ce:section.ce:para:
After 10 min of dosing, the blood sample was collected from the treated and control group mice. The blood samples were then centrifuged, and the supernatant was subjected to lyophilization for the ease in SEM-coupled EDS analysis. As evidenced by Fig. 12
, the blood serum of the treated groups (1 mg/kg and 10 mg/kg) exhibited a noticeable increase in nitrogen content compared to the control group. This finding confirms the presence of NCQDs in the bloodstream of mice. Moreover, a persistent and reproducible blue FL was observed ( Fig. 11
(l)) when the centrifuged blood sample was examined under UV irradiation at 365 nm.

Scientific tactics for assessing the safety and effectiveness of bioprobes in managing various ailments involve assessing their in-vivo toxicity, especially on vital organs. Fig. 13
displays the H&E staining profile of the major organs of mice treated with NCQDs (1 mg/kg and 10 mg/kg) and PBS (control) at 7 and 14 days [93,94]. Encouragingly, the photomicrographs of the major organs showed no observable pathological abnormalities in both the control and treated groups. The normal appearance of the central vein and hepatocytes was evidenced in liver tissue with no indications of inflammatory infiltration. The normal glomeruli, blood vessels, and renal tubules of the kidney sections were observed in each group with no evidence of necrosis. Moreover, the spleen exhibited a normal distribution of white and red pulp, indicating the typical and healthy arrangement of lymphoid tissue and blood-filtering components in its structure [95,96]. The hippocampus region of the brain illustrated no sign of inflammatory reactions, underscoring the robust biological safety of pristine QDs in-vivo.

In summary, this research realized the precise and predictable fabrication of MHA-derived “luminous pearls” with tunable brightness and photostability under the guidance of GRA-coupled ANN. Spherical NCQDs strategically formulated via the HTC route demonstrate a cradle-to-gate approach in the avenue of cancer imaging. The influence of different synthesis parameters on the NCQDs performances was explored via GRA (statistical) and ANN (soft-computing) approaches. GRA inferred Vwater as the critical reaction parameter involved in the pristine fabrication of NCQDs. 5-16-3 ANN architecture discovered almost R2 of one (∼1) and MSE of 3.21e-27, depicting the model’s versatility in deciding the NCQDs characteristics even under the out-of-bound reaction conditions. The physicochemical attributes of NCQDs were probed using TEM, DLS, XRD, FTIR, XPS, UV–Vis, and PL techniques. The “blue-sky” OE sample (HT=190 ˚C, RT=10 h, MMHA=400 mg, Vwater =60 mL, and VEDA=7 mL) exhibited the smallest particle size (3.12 nm) with remarkable advantages encompassing exceptional QY, small PDI, and convincing CO The luminescence emission of the NCQDs exhibits unwavering resilience against photobleaching with no traces of the blinking effect. The haemocompability assays further evidenced the insignificant impact of NCQDs’ particle size on the RBCs morphology even at higher concentration ranges. It is worth noting that OE samples demonstrated vivid and ultrafast multi-color in-vitro and ex-vivo imaging, especially penetrating BBB with a high degree of reproducibility. Furthermore, in-vivo toxicological analysis showed no acute toxicity or inflammation in the vital organs for experimental dosages of 1 and 10 mg/kg up to 14 days. Notably, the convergence of nanoscience and AI holds the scope for the pristine fabrication of desired bioprobes destined for imaging-guided cancer therapy and treatment monitoring.

Priyanka Dash: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.
